local_names.romanized_local: Gān xiānggū (Mandarin); Hoshi shiitake (Japanese); Mallin pyogobeoseot (Korean)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301665 covers shiitake mushroom broadly; no dedicated dried-form code identified. Closest parent match used; dried preparation documented in classification.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LQ covers shiitake mushroom as a category; no distinct dried-form facet code confirmed. Closest parent match used with dried-preparation noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412061001 covers shiitake mushroom substance; no dried-specific descendant confirmed in public browser. Closest parent match used.

serving.description: 1 small handful dried (approx. 10 g), yielding ~50 g rehydrated
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried shiitake mushroom is approximately 10 g (about 3–5 dried caps), which rehydrates to roughly 50 g of cooked mushroom.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Shiitake Mushroom — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried shiitake mushroom (cr_id F14050003). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mushrooms — Analogous estimate based on published low-GI values for dried shiitake and edible mushrooms; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried shiitake mushroom was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on published values for edible mushrooms and shiitake in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). High dietary fibre (11.5 g/100 g) and low sugar (2.21 g/100 g) support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (28 × 7.49 g per 10 g serving) / 100 ≈ 2.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally low sodium (13 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (1534 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Dried shiitake mushroom is highly suitable for hypertensive patients due to its very low sodium content (13 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (1534 mg/100 g); patients should avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (11.5 g/100 g) slows glucose absorption; low sugar content (2.21 g/100 g); low estimated GI (~28); carbohydrate density drops substantially upon rehydration
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried shiitake mushroom has a low estimated glycemic index (~28) and high fibre content (11.5 g/100 g), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; portion control is important as the dried form concentrates carbohydrates (75.4 g/100 g), whereas the rehydrated form has approximately 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.99 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (11.5 g/100 g) supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried shiitake mushroom is cardioprotective due to its very low fat content (0.99 g/100 g), confirmed zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre (11.5 g/100 g) which supports LDL cholesterol reduction, and high potassium (1534 mg/100 g) which supports arterial vasodilation; it is suitable as a regular component of a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan content modulates immune response and reduces inflammatory cytokines; high fibre supports gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shiitake mushrooms contain beta-glucans that modulate immune response and may reduce inflammatory cytokines relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; rehydrate thoroughly before use and avoid cooking in high-saturated-fat oils to preserve anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried shiitake mushroom (Lentinula edodes) is a dehydrated form of the shiitake mushroom, one of the most widely consumed edible fungi in East Asian cuisine. The drying process concentrates flavour compounds, particularly guanosine 5'-monophosphate (GMP), which intensifies the characteristic deep umami taste. Dried shiitake have dark brown, wrinkled caps with a firm, leathery texture that softens upon rehydration in warm water for 20–30 minutes. They are a staple ingredient in Chinese, Japanese, and Korean cooking, used in soups, stews, stir-fries, braised dishes, and rice preparations. Nutritionally, dried shiitake are energy-dense at 296 kcal per 100 g due to carbohydrate concentration from dehydration (75.4 g/100 g), but the high dietary fibre content (11.5 g/100 g) significantly moderates glycemic impact, yielding an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 28. Protein content is 9.58 g/100 g and fat is very low at 0.99 g/100 g with zero cholesterol. Potassium is exceptionally high at 1534 mg/100 g, making dried shiitake an outstanding food for cardiovascular and blood pressure management. Sodium is negligible at 13 mg/100 g. The mushrooms are suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, dried shiitake are beneficial for hypertension (low sodium, high potassium DASH profile), type 2 diabetes (low GI, high fibre, low sugar at 2.21 g/100 g), and cardiovascular disease (zero cholesterol, high fibre for LDL reduction). Beta-glucan polysaccharides present in shiitake have immunomodulatory properties relevant to inflammatory conditions. When rehydrated, the carbohydrate density drops to approximately 6 g/100 g, making portion assessment in the rehydrated form more practical for clinical dietary guidance.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14050003), an approved primary regional source, with confidence 0.95 on all macros and secondary nutrients. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for dried shiitake; this is documented transparently.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dried shiitake mushroom is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F14050003) as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11238 — Mushrooms, shiitake, dried) contains some of these values and may be used for supplementary enrichment in a future pass. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; mushrooms contain no dietary cholesterol (naturally zero). GI of 28 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for dried shiitake mushroom was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is not applicable to mushrooms. Rehydration note: per 100 g dried weight concentrates nutrients significantly; rehydrated shiitake has approximately 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is more relevant for clinical portion guidance.
